explanation.rule: These noun suffixes are very common in academic and formal English. They often derive from verbs or adjectives.

groups.note: Added to verbs to form nouns describing a process or result

examples.definition: the process of changing to suit a new situation
a feeling of respect and approval

groups.note: Added to adjectives to form nouns expressing a state or quality

examples.definition: the state of being fully developed
the state of having great variety

groups.note: Added to verbs or adjectives to form nouns

examples.definition: the quality of continuing despite difficulty

wordForms.definition: the process of changing to suit a new situation
wordForms.example: The film is an adaptation of a bestselling novel.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to change in order to deal with new conditions

wordForms.definition: a feeling of respect and approval
wordForms.example: She looked at the painting with great admiration.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to respect and approve of someone or something

wordForms.example: The dress needed a small alteration before the wedding.

wordForms.definition: a group of people with shared interests
wordForms.example: She joined the local residents' association.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to connect two things in your mind

wordForms.example: The company's domination of the market worried regulators.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to have power and control over others

wordForms.definition: the ability to form mental images or ideas
wordForms.rootDefinition: to form a picture or idea in your mind

wordForms.example: The new software has the capability to process large files.

wordForms.definition: the state of being fully developed
wordForms.example: She showed great maturity in handling the difficult situation.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to be fully grown or developed

wordForms.definition: the state of being available or ready to use
wordForms.example: Please check the availability of the hotel rooms online.
wordForms.rootDefinition: ready or able to be used or obtained

wordForms.definition: the act of keeping away from something
wordForms.example: Tax avoidance is legal, but tax evasion is not.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to stay away from or not do something

wordForms.example: The scientist's brilliance was recognised worldwide.

wordForms.definition: the quality of continuing despite difficulties
wordForms.example: Her persistence paid off when she finally got the job.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to continue doing something despite problems

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: The film is a brilliant ___ of the classic novel.
Her ___ for art led her to study at a famous college.
The dress required only a small ___ to fit her perfectly.
The ___ of flights is limited during the holiday period.
It was his ___ that helped him succeed despite many setbacks.
The young player showed impressive ___ for someone her age.

transformExercises.sentence: The ___ of the novel into a screenplay took two years. (ADAPT)
He was full of ___ for his mentor's dedication. (ADMIRE)
The ___ of resources is critical in emergency planning. (AVAILABLE)
The team's ___ of the market gave them a huge advantage. (DOMINATE)
Creative ___ is a skill that employers value greatly. (IMAGINE)
Her ___ in the face of rejection inspired everyone around her. (PERSIST)
